entries.value: The chip label needs at least 3:1 contrast with the background.
Ensure sufficient color contrast between chip container and its content (text/icon) so that users with low vision can distinguish them
Chips should stay in the same position when selected, as the movement of reordering the list based on selection would add too much cognitive load for users.
With filter chips, if a new chip is added from a bottom sheet or dropdown, it should appear at the front of the list. But the rest of the order should stay the same.
Chips help people enter information, make selections, filter content, or trigger actions. They're best used to help people accomplish their current task faster and easier.
Always use chips in a set — they should never stand alone.
Try to optimise the list, by putting the most frequently selected chip at the start.
Don't let the list of chips get too long.
Chips help people enter information, make selections, filter content, or trigger actions
Chips allow users to filter content or make a choice from a set of options within a compact area.
Use chips to present contextual, supplemental options
Use buttons for the final step in a task
Use an outline to define the edge of the chip's container on regular backgrounds
Chips may use elevation when placed on an image
Avoid replacing major actions with chips. Actions that progress people to the next or previous step should always be displayed as buttons.
Avoid using chips to finish or progress a task
Don’t display a single chip by itself. Chips should appear in a set.
